[[en:Saho-hime]][[fa:ساهو-هیمه]][[ja:狭穂姫命]][[zh:狹穗姬]] [[en:Saho-hime]][[fa:ساهو-هیمه]][[ja:狭穂姫命]][[zh:狹穗姬]] [[en:Saho-hime]][[fa:ساهو-هیمه]][[ja:狭穂姫命]][[zh:狹穗姬]] {{Infobox person|image=File:Kamitsukeno Yatsunada attacking Saohime's castle..jpg|gender={{ill|female|WD=Q6581072}}|nationality={{ill|Yamato Kinship|WD=Q2658252}}|title={{ill|Empress of Japan|WD=Q595038}}|spouse={{ill|Suinin|WD=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}}|children={{ill|Prince Homutsuwake|WD=Q11631423}}|father={{ill|Hikoimasu|WD=Q11487910}}|mother={{ill|Q13360104|WD=Q13360104}}|relatives={{ill|Prince Sahohiko|WD=Q11571755}}; {{ill|Q11626514|WD=Q11626514}}; {{ill|Tanba no Michinushi|WD=Q11368587}}; {{ill|Yamashiro-no-Otsutsukimawaka|WD=Q11466268}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11571754]] Wikidata name: Empress Sahohime [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Legendary empress consort of Japan, goddess of spring}} {{Unreliable sources|date=August 2024}} {{Infobox royalty | consort = yes | name = Saho-hime
{{nobold|{{lang|ja|狭穂姫命}}}} | image = Tsukioka-yoshitoshi-kamitsukeno-yatsunada-attacking-v0-t5oyp9q1amwb1.jpg | alt = | caption = Saho-hime burning in her brother's palace (by {{ill|Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|en|Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|WD=Q467337|lt=Tsukioka Yoshitoshi}}) | succession = {{ill|Empress of Japan|en|Empress of Japan|WD=Q595038|lt=Empress of Japan}} | reign = 28–25 BC | birth_date = ? | death_date = 25 BC | spouse = {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|WD=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin}} | issue = {{ill|Homutsuwake no Mikoto|ja|誉津別命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homutsuwake no Mikoto|qq=}} | father = {{ill|Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu|ja|彦坐王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikoimasu|qq=}} | mother = {{ill|Saho no Ōkuramito-me|ja|沙本之大闇見戸売|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saho no Ōkuramito-me|qq=}} | religion = [[Shinto]] }} '''Saho-hime'''{{efn|Saho-hime's name is also spelt as Sahobime, or Sahajihime.}} (狭穂姫命) was the legendary empress consort of Japan from 28 BC to 25 BC.Anston, p. 166 (Vol. 1)Anston, pp. 170–173 (Vol. 1) == Life == In both the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and the ''[[Kojiki]]'', she was a granddaughter of {{ill|Emperor Kaika|en|Emperor Kaika|WD=Q333548|lt=Emperor Kaika}} and gave birth to one son who, according to the ''Nihon Shoki'' could not speak. Saho-hime died during a rebellion caused by her older brother, Sahohiko. Sahohiko tried to persuade her to kill her husband Emperor Suinin. He asked her whom she loved more, him or the Emperor. When she said the Emperor he reminded her that he had other mistresses. This plan worked and she agreed to kill Emperor Suinin. Saho-hime almost killed the Emperor but she cried and her tears woke the Emperor up. She revealed the plot by her brother to him out of guilt.Yasumaro. O, translated by Gustav Heldt. (2014) “Kojiki. An Account of Ancient Matters”. New York: Columbia University Press. Infuriated that his brother-in-law tried to make his wife kill him, the Emperor sent his troops to his brother-in-law's palace to take his life.{{Cite book |last=Aston |first=William |author-link=William George Aston |url=https://en.m.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_VI |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |publisher=Society}} == The ''Nihon Shoki''{{'}}s account of her demise == After her husband sent troops to her brother's castle, in the ''Nihon Shoki'', Saho-hime went to her brother's palace with their son out of guilt for attempting to kill the Emperor, and refused to leave until it burned down. As it was burning, her brother escaped, along with Saho-hime's son but the Empress died. == The ''Kojiki''{{'}}s account of her demise == In the ''Kojiki'', after Emperor Suinin woke up and Saho-hime went to her brother's castle she gave birth. The Emperor ordered his guards to turn away until she gave birth, and then retrieve her. While the palace was still burning she gave birth.Chamberlain, B. H. (1932) “Translation of the Kojiki.” Kobe: J.L. Thompson & Co. As the Emperor's men came to retrieve Saho-hime's newborn, the child was placed outside the fortress and Saho-hime shaved her head. As the men took Saho-hime, her clothes fell off, as did her hair allowing her to escape. The Emperor was angered and as she was escaping asked her to choose a name for the child. She chose the name Homutsuwake (fire-possessing lord) as the palace was burning whilst she was giving birth. Whilst she and her brother were escaping Suinin killed her brother and Saho-hime herself. == Legacy == It is believed Saho-hime is the deity that lives on Mount Saho and is the goddess of spring.{{Cite web |last=内田圓学 |date=2020-06-17 |title=知っておきたい和歌の女神さま！ 「佐保姫」「竜田姫」「衣通姫」そして「宇治の橋姫」 |url=https://wakadokoro.com/en/learn/zatsu/%E7%9F%A5%E3%81%A3%E3%81%A6%E3%81%8A%E3%81%8D%E3%81%9F%E3%81%84%E5%92%8C%E6%AD%8C%E3%81%AE%E5%A5%B3%E7%A5%9E%E3%81%95%E3%81%BE%EF%BC%81-%E3%80%8C%E4%BD%90%E4%BF%9D%E5%A7%AB%E3%80%8D%E3%80%8C%E7%AB%9C/ |access-date=2024-03-07 |website=二条流 令和和歌所 |language=en-US}} There is a poem attributed to {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|en|Emperor Go-Toba|WD=Q316658|lt=Emperor Go-Toba}} about her: {{quote|"The hazy clothes of Princess Saho are faded, and the flower brocades are quickly faded"}} == Family tree == {{Generations of Jimmu}} == See also == *{{ill|List of Japanese deities|en|List of Japanese deities|WD=Q3240847|lt=List of Japanese deities}} == Notes == {{reflist|group=lower-alpha}} == References == {{reflist}} {{s-start}} {{s-roy|jp}} {{s-bef|before=[[Mimaki-hime]]}} {{s-ttl|title={{ill|Empress of Japan|en|Empress of Japan|WD=Q595038|lt=Empress of Japan}}|years=28–25 BC}} {{s-aft|after=[[Hibasu-hime]]}} {{s-end}} {{Consorts of Japan}} [[en:Saho-hime]] [[fa:ساهو-هیمه]] [[ja:狭穂姫命]] [[zh:狹穗姬]] {{wikidata link|Q11571754}} [[Category:25 BC deaths]] [[Category:Japanese empresses consort]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

As the men took Saho-hime, her clothes fell off, as did her hair allowing her to escape. The Emperor was angered and as she was escaping asked her to choose a name for the child. She chose the name Homutsuwake (fire-possessing lord) as the palace was burning whilst she was giving birth. Whilst she and her brother were escaping Suinin killed her brother and Saho-hime herself. == Legacy == It is believed Saho-hime is the deity that lives on Mount Saho and is the goddess of spring.{{Cite web |last=内田圓学 |date=2020-06-17 |title=知っておきたい和歌の女神さま！ 「佐保姫」「竜田姫」「衣通姫」そして「宇治の橋姫」 |url=https://wakadokoro.com/en/learn/zatsu/%E7%9F%A5%E3%81%A3%E3%81%A6%E3%81%8A%E3%81%8D%E3%81%9F%E3%81%84%E5%92%8C%E6%AD%8C%E3%81%AE%E5%A5%B3%E7%A5%9E%E3%81%95%E3%81%BE%EF%BC%81-%E3%80%8C%E4%BD%90%E4%BF%9D%E5%A7%AB%E3%80%8D%E3%80%8C%E7%AB%9C/ |access-date=2024-03-07 |website=二条流 令和和歌所 |language=en-US}} There is a poem attributed to {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|en|Emperor Go-Toba|WD=Q316658|lt=Emperor Go-Toba}} about her: {{quote|"The hazy clothes of Princess Saho are faded, and the flower brocades are quickly faded"}} == Family tree == {{Generations of Jimmu}} == See also == *{{ill|List of Japanese deities|en|List of Japanese deities|WD=Q3240847|lt=List of Japanese deities}} == Notes == {{reflist|group=lower-alpha}} == References == {{reflist}} {{s-start}} {{s-roy|jp}} {{s-bef|before=[[Mimaki-hime]]}} {{s-ttl|title={{ill|Empress of Japan|en|Empress of Japan|WD=Q595038|lt=Empress of Japan}}|years=28–25 BC}} {{s-aft|after=[[Hibasu-hime]]}} {{s-end}} {{Consorts of Japan}} [[en:Saho-hime]] [[fa:ساهو-هیمه]] [[ja:狭穂姫命]] [[zh:狹穗姬]] {{wikidata link|Q11571754}} [[Category:25 BC deaths]] [[Category:Japanese empresses consort]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

revision.text:
[[en:Saho-hime]][[fa:ساهو-هیمه]][[ja:狭穂姫命]][[zh:狹穗姬]] [[en:Saho-hime]][[fa:ساهو-هیمه]][[ja:狭穂姫命]][[zh:狹穗姬]] [[en:Saho-hime]][[fa:ساهو-هیمه]][[ja:狭穂姫命]][[zh:狹穗姬]] {{Infobox person|image=File:Kamitsukeno Yatsunada attacking Saohime's castle..jpg|gender={{ill|female|WD=Q6581072}}|nationality={{ill|Yamato Kinship|WD=Q2658252}}|title={{ill|Empress of Japan|WD=Q595038}}|spouse={{ill|Suinin|WD=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}}|children={{ill|Prince Homutsuwake|WD=Q11631423}}|father={{ill|Hikoimasu|WD=Q11487910}}|mother={{ill|Q13360104|WD=Q13360104}}|relatives={{ill|Prince Sahohiko|WD=Q11571755}}; {{ill|Q11626514|WD=Q11626514}}; {{ill|Tanba no Michinushi|WD=Q11368587}}; {{ill|Yamashiro-no-Otsutsukimawaka|WD=Q11466268}}}} {{Short description|Legendary empress consort of Japan, goddess of spring}} {{Unreliable sources|date=August 2024}} {{Infobox royalty | consort = yes | name = Saho-hime
{{nobold|{{lang|ja|狭穂姫命}}}} | image = Tsukioka-yoshitoshi-kamitsukeno-yatsunada-attacking-v0-t5oyp9q1amwb1.jpg | alt = | caption = Saho-hime burning in her brother's palace (by {{ill|Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|en|Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|WD=Q467337|lt=Tsukioka Yoshitoshi}}) | succession = {{ill|Empress of Japan|en|Empress of Japan|WD=Q595038|lt=Empress of Japan}} | reign = 28–25 BC | birth_date = ? | death_date = 25 BC | spouse = {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|WD=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin}} | issue = {{ill|Homutsuwake no Mikoto|ja|誉津別命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homutsuwake no Mikoto|qq=}} | father = {{ill|Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu|ja|彦坐王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikoimasu|qq=}} | mother = {{ill|Saho no Ōkuramito-me|ja|沙本之大闇見戸売|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saho no Ōkuramito-me|qq=}} | religion = [[Shinto]] }} '''Saho-hime'''{{efn|Saho-hime's name is also spelt as Sahobime, or Sahajihime.}} (狭穂姫命) was the legendary empress consort of Japan from 28 BC to 25 BC.Anston, p. 166 (Vol. 1)Anston, pp. 170–173 (Vol. 1) == Life == In both the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and the ''[[Kojiki]]'', she was a granddaughter of {{ill|Emperor Kaika|en|Emperor Kaika|WD=Q333548|lt=Emperor Kaika}} and gave birth to one son who, according to the ''Nihon Shoki'' could not speak. Saho-hime died during a rebellion caused by her older brother, Sahohiko. Sahohiko tried to persuade her to kill her husband Emperor Suinin. He asked her whom she loved more, him or the Emperor. When she said the Emperor he reminded her that he had other mistresses. This plan worked and she agreed to kill Emperor Suinin. Saho-hime almost killed the Emperor but she cried and her tears woke the Emperor up. She revealed the plot by her brother to him out of guilt.Yasumaro. O, translated by Gustav Heldt. (2014) “Kojiki. An Account of Ancient Matters”. New York: Columbia University Press. Infuriated that his brother-in-law tried to make his wife kill him, the Emperor sent his troops to his brother-in-law's palace to take his life.{{Cite book |last=Aston |first=William |author-link=William George Aston |url=https://en.m.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_VI |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |publisher=Society}} == The ''Nihon Shoki''{{'}}s account of her demise == After her husband sent troops to her brother's castle, in the ''Nihon Shoki'', Saho-hime went to her brother's palace with their son out of guilt for attempting to kill the Emperor, and refused to leave until it burned down. As it was burning, her brother escaped, along with Saho-hime's son but the Empress died. == The ''Kojiki''{{'}}s account of her demise == In the ''Kojiki'', after Emperor Suinin woke up and Saho-hime went to her brother's castle she gave birth. The Emperor ordered his guards to turn away until she gave birth, and then retrieve her. While the palace was still burning she gave birth.Chamberlain, B. H. (1932) “Translation of the Kojiki.” Kobe: J.L. Thompson & Co. As the Emperor's men came to retrieve Saho-hime's newborn, the child was placed outside the fortress and Saho-hime shaved her head. As the men took Saho-hime, her clothes fell off, as did her hair allowing her to escape. The Emperor was angered and as she was escaping asked her to choose a name for the child. She chose the name Homutsuwake (fire-possessing lord) as the palace was burning whilst she was giving birth. Whilst she and her brother were escaping Suinin killed her brother and Saho-hime herself. == Legacy == It is believed Saho-hime is the deity that lives on Mount Saho and is the goddess of spring.{{Cite web |last=内田圓学 |date=2020-06-17 |title=知っておきたい和歌の女神さま！ 「佐保姫」「竜田姫」「衣通姫」そして「宇治の橋姫」 |url=https://wakadokoro.com/en/learn/zatsu/%E7%9F%A5%E3%81%A3%E3%81%A6%E3%81%8A%E3%81%8D%E3%81%9F%E3%81%84%E5%92%8C%E6%AD%8C%E3%81%AE%E5%A5%B3%E7%A5%9E%E3%81%95%E3%81%BE%EF%BC%81-%E3%80%8C%E4%BD%90%E4%BF%9D%E5%A7%AB%E3%80%8D%E3%80%8C%E7%AB%9C/ |access-date=2024-03-07 |website=二条流 令和和歌所 |language=en-US}} There is a poem attributed to {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|en|Emperor Go-Toba|WD=Q316658|lt=Emperor Go-Toba}} about her: {{quote|"The hazy clothes of Princess Saho are faded, and the flower brocades are quickly faded"}} == Family tree == {{Generations of Jimmu}} == See also == *{{ill|List of Japanese deities|en|List of Japanese deities|WD=Q3240847|lt=List of Japanese deities}} == Notes == {{reflist|group=lower-alpha}} == References == {{reflist}} {{s-start}} {{s-roy|jp}} {{s-bef|before=[[Mimaki-hime]]}} {{s-ttl|title={{ill|Empress of Japan|en|Empress of Japan|WD=Q595038|lt=Empress of Japan}}|years=28–25 BC}} {{s-aft|after=[[Hibasu-hime]]}} {{s-end}} {{Consorts of Japan}} [[en:Saho-hime]] [[fa:ساهو-هیمه]] [[ja:狭穂姫命]] [[zh:狹穗姬]] {{wikidata link|Q11571754}} [[Category:25 BC deaths]] [[Category:Japanese empresses consort]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

[[en:Saho-hime]][[fa:ساهو-هیمه]][[ja:狭穂姫命]][[zh:狹穗姬]] [[en:Saho-hime]][[fa:ساهو-هیمه]][[ja:狭穂姫命]][[zh:狹穗姬]] [[en:Saho-hime]][[fa:ساهو-هیمه]][[ja:狭穂姫命]][[zh:狹穗姬]] {{Infobox person|image=File:Kamitsukeno Yatsunada attacking Saohime's castle..jpg|gender={{ill|female|WD=Q6581072|1=Female (gender)}}|nationality={{ill|Yamato Kinship|WD=Q2658252|1=Yamato Kingship}}|title={{ill|Empress of Japan|WD=Q595038|1=Empress of Japan}}|spouse={{ill|Suinin|WD=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}}|children={{ill|Prince Homutsuwake|WD=Q11631423|1=Homutsuwake no Mikoto}}|father={{ill|Hikoimasu|WD=Q11487910|1=Hikoimasu no Ōkimi}}|mother={{ill|Q13360104|WD=Q13360104|1=Saho no Ōkuramito-me}}|relatives={{ill|Prince Sahohiko|WD=Q11571755|1=Sahohiko no Mikoto}}; {{ill|Q11626514|WD=Q11626514|1=Ozaho no Ōkimi}}; {{ill|Tanba no Michinushi|WD=Q11368587|1=Tanba-no-Michinushi-no-Mikoto}}; {{ill|Yamashiro-no-Otsutsukimawaka|WD=Q11466268|1=Yamashiro-no-Otsutsukimawaka}}}} {{Short description|Legendary empress consort of Japan, goddess of spring}} {{Unreliable sources|date=August 2024}} {{Infobox royalty | consort = yes | name = Saho-hime
{{nobold|{{lang|ja|狭穂姫命}}}} | image = Tsukioka-yoshitoshi-kamitsukeno-yatsunada-attacking-v0-t5oyp9q1amwb1.jpg | alt = | caption = Saho-hime burning in her brother's palace (by {{ill|Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|en|Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|WD=Q467337|lt=Tsukioka Yoshitoshi|1=Tsukioka Yoshitoshi}}) | succession = {{ill|Empress of Japan|en|Empress of Japan|WD=Q595038|lt=Empress of Japan|1=Empress of Japan}} | reign = 28–25 BC | birth_date = ? | death_date = 25 BC | spouse = {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|WD=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin|1=Emperor Suinin}} | issue = {{ill|Homutsuwake no Mikoto|ja|誉津別命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homutsuwake no Mikoto|qq=}} | father = {{ill|Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu|ja|彦坐王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikoimasu|qq=}} | mother = {{ill|Saho no Ōkuramito-me|ja|沙本之大闇見戸売|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saho no Ōkuramito-me|qq=}} | religion = [[Shinto]] }} '''Saho-hime'''{{efn|Saho-hime's name is also spelt as Sahobime, or Sahajihime.}} (狭穂姫命) was the legendary empress consort of Japan from 28 BC to 25 BC.Anston, p. 166 (Vol. 1)Anston, pp. 170–173 (Vol. 1) == Life == In both the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' and the ''[[Kojiki]]'', she was a granddaughter of {{ill|Emperor Kaika|en|Emperor Kaika|WD=Q333548|lt=Emperor Kaika|1=Emperor Kaika}} and gave birth to one son who, according to the ''Nihon Shoki'' could not speak. Saho-hime died during a rebellion caused by her older brother, Sahohiko. Sahohiko tried to persuade her to kill her husband Emperor Suinin. He asked her whom she loved more, him or the Emperor. When she said the Emperor he reminded her that he had other mistresses. This plan worked and she agreed to kill Emperor Suinin. Saho-hime almost killed the Emperor but she cried and her tears woke the Emperor up. She revealed the plot by her brother to him out of guilt.Yasumaro. O, translated by Gustav Heldt. (2014) “Kojiki. An Account of Ancient Matters”. New York: Columbia University Press. Infuriated that his brother-in-law tried to make his wife kill him, the Emperor sent his troops to his brother-in-law's palace to take his life.{{Cite book |last=Aston |first=William |author-link=William George Aston |url=https://en.m.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_VI |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |publisher=Society}} == The ''Nihon Shoki''{{'}}s account of her demise == After her husband sent troops to her brother's castle, in the ''Nihon Shoki'', Saho-hime went to her brother's palace with their son out of guilt for attempting to kill the Emperor, and refused to leave until it burned down. As it was burning, her brother escaped, along with Saho-hime's son but the Empress died. == The ''Kojiki''{{'}}s account of her demise == In the ''Kojiki'', after Emperor Suinin woke up and Saho-hime went to her brother's castle she gave birth. The Emperor ordered his guards to turn away until she gave birth, and then retrieve her. While the palace was still burning she gave birth.Chamberlain, B. H. (1932) “Translation of the Kojiki.” Kobe: J.L. Thompson & Co. As the Emperor's men came to retrieve Saho-hime's newborn, the child was placed outside the fortress and Saho-hime shaved her head. As the men took Saho-hime, her clothes fell off, as did her hair allowing her to escape. The Emperor was angered and as she was escaping asked her to choose a name for the child. She chose the name Homutsuwake (fire-possessing lord) as the palace was burning whilst she was giving birth. Whilst she and her brother were escaping Suinin killed her brother and Saho-hime herself. == Legacy == It is believed Saho-hime is the deity that lives on Mount Saho and is the goddess of spring.{{Cite web |last=内田圓学 |date=2020-06-17 |title=知っておきたい和歌の女神さま！ 「佐保姫」「竜田姫」「衣通姫」そして「宇治の橋姫」 |url=https://wakadokoro.com/en/learn/zatsu/%E7%9F%A5%E3%81%A3%E3%81%A6%E3%81%8A%E3%81%8D%E3%81%9F%E3%81%84%E5%92%8C%E6%AD%8C%E3%81%AE%E5%A5%B3%E7%A5%9E%E3%81%95%E3%81%BE%EF%BC%81-%E3%80%8C%E4%BD%90%E4%BF%9D%E5%A7%AB%E3%80%8D%E3%80%8C%E7%AB%9C/ |access-date=2024-03-07 |website=二条流 令和和歌所 |language=en-US}} There is a poem attributed to {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|en|Emperor Go-Toba|WD=Q316658|lt=Emperor Go-Toba|1=Emperor Go-Toba}} about her: {{quote|"The hazy clothes of Princess Saho are faded, and the flower brocades are quickly faded"}} == Family tree == {{Generations of Jimmu}} == See also == *{{ill|List of Japanese deities|en|List of Japanese deities|WD=Q3240847|lt=List of Japanese deities|1=List of Japanese deities}} == Notes == {{reflist|group=lower-alpha}} == References == {{reflist}} {{s-start}} {{s-roy|jp}} {{s-bef|before=[[Mimaki-hime]]}} {{s-ttl|title={{ill|Empress of Japan|en|Empress of Japan|WD=Q595038|lt=Empress of Japan|1=Empress of Japan}}|years=28–25 BC}} {{s-aft|after=[[Hibasu-hime]]}} {{s-end}} {{Consorts of Japan}} [[en:Saho-hime]] [[fa:ساهو-هیمه]] [[ja:狭穂姫命]] [[zh:狹穗姬]] {{wikidata link|Q11571754}} [[Category:25 BC deaths]] [[Category:Japanese empresses consort]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]
